sources.notes: Hard version keeps the same inversion-graph bipartiteness condition with larger n.
Official reference code optimizes the two-threshold DP using Fenwick trees over the second threshold.

annotation.summary: Count subsequences whose inversion graph is bipartite for the harder constraints.
annotation.constraints: n is large enough that the easy cubic DP must be optimized; O(n^2 log n) style value-state aggregation is acceptable.
annotation.core_idea: Use the same structural fact as the easy version: selected subsequences must avoid a strictly decreasing subsequence of length 3. The DP state is still the pair of thresholds (current maximum, largest second element in a decreasing pair), but transitions that sum over many prior states are aggregated with Fenwick trees, turning the counting recurrence into range-sum queries and updates.
annotation.complexity: O(n^2 log n) per test case.

solution_variants.name: fenwick-optimized-two-threshold-dp
solution_variants.summary: Maintain the two-threshold subsequence DP with Fenwick trees to aggregate valid previous states.

tags.evidence: The main solution is a subsequence-counting DP over order thresholds.
Fenwick trees are required to make the hard-version DP transitions efficient enough.
The quantity counted is the number of valid subsequences satisfying a graph/order-pattern constraint.
Bipartiteness of the inversion graph is solved through an equivalent forbidden decreasing-triple structure.
